GitHub Organizations and Team Management for Open Source Projects

If you're a developer, you'll end up touching open source — the world's moved that way, and it's a great way to experience real team collaboration. And if you ever build something open source that grows beyond a one-person project, you'll need to know how Organizations and Teams work on GitHub.
An Organization is basically the GitHub equivalent of a company — you create the company, then host projects under it. How do you create one?
Open GitHub, click your avatar in the top-right menu, find:
You land on the Organizations page (lists Orgs you belong to). Click "New Organization":
Looks like a pricing page next — but you don't have to pay. Pick the
Heads up — "Organization account name" affects both your Org name and its URL. Don't put garbage there.
GitHub will ask about inviting members and what you're interested in — you can skip those. Once you see this screen, your Organization is set up:
Now you can push open source repos to it and add Teams.
Org's set up — onto the project side. The typical open source flow looks like:
Fork → fix locally → push → open PR → maintainer reviews → merge
For an Org-owned project, it's better to use a Team to manage members and invites. Reasons:
Auto-assign random reviewers when team members open PRs
And more. Teams work well for distributed contributors.
I'll create a test repo called
You can manage user access directly per-repo via "Manage access", but the better way is using a Team. Click the "Teams" tab at the top:
Fill in the team details and name:
Now add a member to the Team. Click "Members" at the top, then "Add a member":
, not Org members. If you're not sure where you are, go back to the Org-level "Teams" tab and click back into your team.
Once added, they show up in Members:
The team member can't modify the
repo yet because we haven't assigned the repo to the team. Switch to "Repositories" and click "Add repository":
Pick the repo to give the team:
You can also choose what level of access this team gets on the repo:
— clone, read the repo, open issues and PRs.
— same as Read, plus manage issues and PRs.
— same as Triage, plus push code.
— same as Write, plus some repo settings.
— full access including all repo settings and collaborator management.
I'll set it to Admin for demo purposes — that team will have the highest level of access on this repo.
Open the test account in another window and you can see the previously-read-only files are now editable:
Without the right permission, only clone and read work — even inline edits in the GitHub UI are blocked:

Any developer will end up touching open source at some point — and if you're running your own project that's growing, knowing how to use GitHub Organizations and Teams becomes pretty important. This post walks through creating a free Organization, building a Team inside it, adding members, assigning repos to the team, and configuring per-team repository roles (Read / Triage / Write / Maintain / Admin).
